title: Septic Arthritis
oneLiner: Acute bacterial joint infection causing rapid cartilage destruction; orthopaedic emergency needing immediate aspiration and antibiotics to prevent sepsis and permanent damage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – this is the mimic that kills fastest.
  - *Single finding:* Crepitus on passive motion, bullae, or systemic toxicity (high fever, hypotension) **out of proportion** to the joint findings. Do not delay surgical review for a joint aspiration if skin changes are present.
- **Acute prosthetic joint infection** (if patient has a joint replacement) – infected hardware can cause periprosthetic fracture or sepsis rapidly.
  - *Single finding:* Pain on weight-bearing or at rest, plus raised inflammatory markers; **do not aspirate through skin over prosthesis** without orthopaedic guidance – risk of introducing infection into deep space.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute monoarticular pain (hours to days) – knee > hip > shoulder > ankle/wrist
  - Inability to bear weight or actively move the joint (pseudoparalysis)
  - Fever and rigors (may be absent in elderly or immunocompromised)
  - Warm, swollen, tender joint with erythema (but may be subtle in hip or shoulder)
  - Younger patients: often preceded by trauma, URTI, or skin infection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Joint held in position of comfort (e.g. hip: flexed, abducted, externally rotated)
  - Marked pain with any passive range of motion (the hallmark)
  - Raised temperature over joint; look for signs of sepsis (tachycardia, hypotension)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Crystal arthropathy (gout/pseudogout):** Pain maximal at rest, often less systemic toxicity, may have tophi/chondrocalcinosis; **splitter:** polarised light microscopy shows crystals.
  - **Reactive arthritis:** Typically oligoarticular, recent GI/GU infection, conjunctivitis, urethritis; lacks joint effusion with high WBC.
  - **Traumatic haemarthrosis:** History of injury, no fever; aspiration shows blood, normal CRP/WCC.
  - **Osteomyelitis (adjacent):** Deeper bone pain, point tenderness over metaphysis, XR may show periosteal reaction; MRI distinguishes.
  - **Cellulitis:** Swelling over joint but **passive range of motion preserved** and joint is not as exquisitely tender – key distinguishing test!.
- **Splitter:** **Preserved passive range of motion** – if the patient can tolerate passive movement, septic arthritis is very unlikely. Test this *before* any analgesia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Urgent orthopaedic review** and arrange **joint aspiration** – sterile procedure; send fluid for: Gram stain, culture & sensitivity, crystal analysis, cell count.
  - Blood cultures (two sets from different sites) **before** antibiotics if not delaying treatment.
  - Urgent point-of-care ultrasound (if available) to guide aspiration.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (raised, but can be normal early)
  - U&Es, creatinine (for antibiotic dosing)
  - Blood cultures (positive in ~50%)
  - Consider HIV, hepatitis serology if high risk
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain X-ray of the joint (to rule out fracture, osteomyelitis, or gas in soft tissues) – but **does not rule out septic arthritis**.
  - **Second-line:** MRI with contrast if XR negative and clinical suspicion is moderate (e.g. hip, spine) but **do not delay aspiration for MRI**.
- **Special tests:**
  - Polarised light microscopy for crystals (gout – needle-shaped, negatively birefringent; pseudogout – rhomboid, positively birefringent).
  - Joint fluid cell count: >50,000 cells/µL (predominantly neutrophils) is highly suggestive but not diagnostic.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - No single validated score is widely used in UK practice; diagnosis is made on **high clinical suspicion + positive aspiration culture**.
- **Never-miss test:** **Joint aspiration** – the definitive test. If you cannot perform it immediately (e.g. hip joint, coagulopathy), do **blood cultures** and start antibiotics, but document urgently planned image-guided aspiration.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **DO NOT DELAY** antibiotics beyond 1 hour if septic arthritis is strongly suspected, even before aspiration results.
  - Contact **orthopaedics on-call** urgently.
  - Analgesia: IV morphine (e.g. 2.5–5 mg) if in severe pain; caution with hypotension.
  - IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) if hypotensive (sepsis).
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Continue **IV antibiotics** (see Drugs section) for 2–4 weeks usually, then oral for 2–6 weeks depending on organism (NICE CG100).
  - Repeat aspiration if re-accumulation; some patients require **arthroscopic lavage** or open washout (ortho decision).
  - Daily NEWS2 monitoring; escalate if ≥5 or signs of worsening sepsis.
  - Consider **physiotherapy** early to prevent adhesions after acute phase.
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Oral antibiotics (guided by sensitivities) for 2–6 weeks total.
  - Orthopaedic follow-up to assess joint function; monitor for osteomyelitis or avascular necrosis.
  - If prosthetic joint: usually needs **two-stage revision** (remove hardware, spacer with antibiotics, later re-implant).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (adjust for age, comorbidities)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if not contraindicated
  - Diet: NBM only if planning surgical intervention; otherwise regular diet
  - Observation: NEWS2 4-hourly initially, increase to 1-hourly if unstable
  - Analgesia: regular paracetamol 1 g QDS PO, + PRN morphine
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (empiric – community-acquired, non-prosthetic):**
  - **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV QDS (every 6 hours) – for Gram-positive coverage (Staph. aureus, strep.)
    - Renal adjustment: no major dose change unless eGFR <10 (see local policy)
  - **Plus** consider second agent if severe or immunocompromised: **Gentamicin** 5–7 mg/kg IV OD (or alternative – [check local policy])
  - **Penicillin allergy:** **Clindamycin** 600 mg IV QDS (or 1.2 g QDS if severe) – covers anaerobes and MRSA is uncommon in community.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Vancomycin** (if MRSA suspected – e.g. recent IVDU, prosthetic joint, hospital-acquired) – dose 15–20 mg/kg IV BD (adjust to trough levels, [check local policy])
  - **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV OD – if Gram-negative suspected (older adult, immunocompromised)
- **Duration:**
  - IV usually 2 weeks then switch to oral guided by sensitivities and clinical response (NICE CG100); total 4–6 weeks.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid in penicillin allergy (true anaphylaxis); caution in liver disease.
  - Gentamicin: monitor renal function and hearing; avoid in myasthenia gravis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: no major change until eGFR <10 (then reduce to 1–2 g QDS [check local policy]).
  - Gentamicin: use adjusted body weight and **always check pre-levels before second dose**.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin** – giving a second dose without checking levels can lead to ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity. **Always check trough level (<1 mg/L) or peak level as per local protocol before second dose.**
  - **Flucloxacillin** – high doses can cause neurotoxicity (especially in renal impairment); do not exceed 12 g daily.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock with multi-organ failure
  - Joint destruction and cartilage loss (can occur within 24–48 hours)
  - Osteomyelitis of adjacent bone
  - Abscess formation (periarticular, psoas if hip infected)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic joint stiffness, erosion, avascular necrosis
  - Secondary osteoarthritis
  - Chronic osteomyelitis (especially with MRSA or fungal)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality** in septic arthritis: ~5–15%, higher in elderly, prosthetic joints, or immunocompromised.
- **Joint function:** delayed treatment (>48 hours from onset) leads to irreversible damage in >50% of survivors. Prompt aspiration and antibiotics improve outcomes significantly.
- Full recovery is possible if treated within 24 hours.
sections.Pearls (Positive Actions):
- **Always perform passive range of motion** before giving strong analgesia – it’s the best clinical differentiator from cellulitis or trauma.
- **Aspirate the joint before antibiotics** unless this would cause a dangerous delay (e.g. >1 hour, or patient is septic). A negative culture after antibiotics makes management difficult.
- **Check for prosthetic joints** in any patient with joint pain – even old ones can be infected years later. Never aspirate through the skin without imaging guidance if possible.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use NSAIDs** to manage pain until septic arthritis is excluded – they can mask the inflammatory response and delay diagnosis.
- **Do not start antibiotics** without at least blood cultures and ideally joint fluid Gram stain – you will lose the culture yield.
- **Do not delay orthopaedic referral** for imaging – XR is normal in early disease; waiting for MRI risks joint destruction.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- *(Note: most patients with confirmed septic arthritis are admitted for IV antibiotics. Safety netting applies to those discharged after a negative culture but with low suspicion – rare.)*
- **Return if:** sudden worsening of joint pain, fever, inability to move the joint, redness spreading, or feeling generally unwell.
- **Follow-up:** orthopaedic outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; repeat CRP and WCC in 72 hours.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEW2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids
  - GCS <15, confusion
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) not responding to 500 mL bolus
  - Patient has a **prosthetic joint** – this requires immediate ortho input and usually joint washout
- **ICU referral if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors
  - Respiratory failure or need for invasive monitoring
  - Multi-organ dysfunction
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest.

rawMarkdown:
## Septic Arthritis
**One-liner:** Acute bacterial joint infection causing rapid cartilage destruction; orthopaedic emergency needing immediate aspiration and antibiotics to prevent sepsis and permanent damage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – this is the mimic that kills fastest.
  - *Single finding:* Crepitus on passive motion, bullae, or systemic toxicity (high fever, hypotension) **out of proportion** to the joint findings. Do not delay surgical review for a joint aspiration if skin changes are present.
- **Acute prosthetic joint infection** (if patient has a joint replacement) – infected hardware can cause periprosthetic fracture or sepsis rapidly.
  - *Single finding:* Pain on weight-bearing or at rest, plus raised inflammatory markers; **do not aspirate through skin over prosthesis** without orthopaedic guidance – risk of introducing infection into deep space.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute monoarticular pain (hours to days) – knee > hip > shoulder > ankle/wrist
  - Inability to bear weight or actively move the joint (pseudoparalysis)
  - Fever and rigors (may be absent in elderly or immunocompromised)
  - Warm, swollen, tender joint with erythema (but may be subtle in hip or shoulder)
  - Younger patients: often preceded by trauma, URTI, or skin infection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Joint held in position of comfort (e.g. hip: flexed, abducted, externally rotated)
  - Marked pain with any passive range of motion (the hallmark)
  - Raised temperature over joint; look for signs of sepsis (tachycardia, hypotension)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Crystal arthropathy (gout/pseudogout):** Pain maximal at rest, often less systemic toxicity, may have tophi/chondrocalcinosis; **splitter:** polarised light microscopy shows crystals.
  - **Reactive arthritis:** Typically oligoarticular, recent GI/GU infection, conjunctivitis, urethritis; lacks joint effusion with high WBC.
  - **Traumatic haemarthrosis:** History of injury, no fever; aspiration shows blood, normal CRP/WCC.
  - **Osteomyelitis (adjacent):** Deeper bone pain, point tenderness over metaphysis, XR may show periosteal reaction; MRI distinguishes.
  - **Cellulitis:** Swelling over joint but **passive range of motion preserved** and joint is not as exquisitely tender – key distinguishing test!.
- **Splitter:** **Preserved passive range of motion** – if the patient can tolerate passive movement, septic arthritis is very unlikely. Test this *before* any analgesia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Urgent orthopaedic review** and arrange **joint aspiration** – sterile procedure; send fluid for: Gram stain, culture & sensitivity, crystal analysis, cell count.
  - Blood cultures (two sets from different sites) **before** antibiotics if not delaying treatment.
  - Urgent point-of-care ultrasound (if available) to guide aspiration.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (raised, but can be normal early)
  - U&Es, creatinine (for antibiotic dosing)
  - Blood cultures (positive in ~50%)
  - Consider HIV, hepatitis serology if high risk
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain X-ray of the joint (to rule out fracture, osteomyelitis, or gas in soft tissues) – but **does not rule out septic arthritis**.
  - **Second-line:** MRI with contrast if XR negative and clinical suspicion is moderate (e.g. hip, spine) but **do not delay aspiration for MRI**.
- **Special tests:**
  - Polarised light microscopy for crystals (gout – needle-shaped, negatively birefringent; pseudogout – rhomboid, positively birefringent).
  - Joint fluid cell count: >50,000 cells/µL (predominantly neutrophils) is highly suggestive but not diagnostic.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - No single validated score is widely used in UK practice; diagnosis is made on **high clinical suspicion + positive aspiration culture**.
- **Never-miss test:** **Joint aspiration** – the definitive test. If you cannot perform it immediately (e.g. hip joint, coagulopathy), do **blood cultures** and start antibiotics, but document urgently planned image-guided aspiration.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **DO NOT DELAY** antibiotics beyond 1 hour if septic arthritis is strongly suspected, even before aspiration results.
  - Contact **orthopaedics on-call** urgently.
  - Analgesia: IV morphine (e.g. 2.5–5 mg) if in severe pain; caution with hypotension.
  - IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) if hypotensive (sepsis).
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Continue **IV antibiotics** (see Drugs section) for 2–4 weeks usually, then oral for 2–6 weeks depending on organism (NICE CG100).
  - Repeat aspiration if re-accumulation; some patients require **arthroscopic lavage** or open washout (ortho decision).
  - Daily NEWS2 monitoring; escalate if ≥5 or signs of worsening sepsis.
  - Consider **physiotherapy** early to prevent adhesions after acute phase.
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Oral antibiotics (guided by sensitivities) for 2–6 weeks total.
  - Orthopaedic follow-up to assess joint function; monitor for osteomyelitis or avascular necrosis.
  - If prosthetic joint: usually needs **two-stage revision** (remove hardware, spacer with antibiotics, later re-implant).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (adjust for age, comorbidities)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if not contraindicated
  - Diet: NBM only if planning surgical intervention; otherwise regular diet
  - Observation: NEWS2 4-hourly initially, increase to 1-hourly if unstable
  - Analgesia: regular paracetamol 1 g QDS PO, + PRN morphine

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (empiric – community-acquired, non-prosthetic):**
  - **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV QDS (every 6 hours) – for Gram-positive coverage (Staph. aureus, strep.)
    - Renal adjustment: no major dose change unless eGFR <10 (see local policy)
  - **Plus** consider second agent if severe or immunocompromised: **Gentamicin** 5–7 mg/kg IV OD (or alternative – [check local policy])
  - **Penicillin allergy:** **Clindamycin** 600 mg IV QDS (or 1.2 g QDS if severe) – covers anaerobes and MRSA is uncommon in community.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Vancomycin** (if MRSA suspected – e.g. recent IVDU, prosthetic joint, hospital-acquired) – dose 15–20 mg/kg IV BD (adjust to trough levels, [check local policy])
  - **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV OD – if Gram-negative suspected (older adult, immunocompromised)
- **Duration:**
  - IV usually 2 weeks then switch to oral guided by sensitivities and clinical response (NICE CG100); total 4–6 weeks.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid in penicillin allergy (true anaphylaxis); caution in liver disease.
  - Gentamicin: monitor renal function and hearing; avoid in myasthenia gravis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: no major change until eGFR <10 (then reduce to 1–2 g QDS [check local policy]).
  - Gentamicin: use adjusted body weight and **always check pre-levels before second dose**.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin** – giving a second dose without checking levels can lead to ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity. **Always check trough level (<1 mg/L) or peak level as per local protocol before second dose.**
  - **Flucloxacillin** – high doses can cause neurotoxicity (especially in renal impairment); do not exceed 12 g daily.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock with multi-organ failure
  - Joint destruction and cartilage loss (can occur within 24–48 hours)
  - Osteomyelitis of adjacent bone
  - Abscess formation (periarticular, psoas if hip infected)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic joint stiffness, erosion, avascular necrosis
  - Secondary osteoarthritis
  - Chronic osteomyelitis (especially with MRSA or fungal)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality** in septic arthritis: ~5–15%, higher in elderly, prosthetic joints, or immunocompromised.
- **Joint function:** delayed treatment (>48 hours from onset) leads to irreversible damage in >50% of survivors. Prompt aspiration and antibiotics improve outcomes significantly.
- Full recovery is possible if treated within 24 hours.

### Pearls (Positive Actions)
- **Always perform passive range of motion** before giving strong analgesia – it’s the best clinical differentiator from cellulitis or trauma.
- **Aspirate the joint before antibiotics** unless this would cause a dangerous delay (e.g. >1 hour, or patient is septic). A negative culture after antibiotics makes management difficult.
- **Check for prosthetic joints** in any patient with joint pain – even old ones can be infected years later. Never aspirate through the skin without imaging guidance if possible.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use NSAIDs** to manage pain until septic arthritis is excluded – they can mask the inflammatory response and delay diagnosis.
- **Do not start antibiotics** without at least blood cultures and ideally joint fluid Gram stain – you will lose the culture yield.
- **Do not delay orthopaedic referral** for imaging – XR is normal in early disease; waiting for MRI risks joint destruction.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- *(Note: most patients with confirmed septic arthritis are admitted for IV antibiotics. Safety netting applies to those discharged after a negative culture but with low suspicion – rare.)*
- **Return if:** sudden worsening of joint pain, fever, inability to move the joint, redness spreading, or feeling generally unwell.
- **Follow-up:** orthopaedic outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; repeat CRP and WCC in 72 hours.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEW2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids
  - GCS <15, confusion
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) not responding to 500 mL bolus
  - Patient has a **prosthetic joint** – this requires immediate ortho input and usually joint washout
- **ICU referral if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors
  - Respiratory failure or need for invasive monitoring
  - Multi-organ dysfunction
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest.
